verses.text: Hezekiah falls sick to death, and Isaiah tells him to set his house in order; the king turns his face to the wall and weeps, pleading his faithfulness. He takes his grief straight to God. Even a settled sentence may be brought before the LORD in prayer.

Hezekiah falls sick to death, and Isaiah tells him to set his house in order; the king turns his face to the wall and weeps, pleading his faithfulness. He takes his grief straight to God. Even a settled sentence may be brought before the LORD in prayer.

Hezekiah falls sick to death, and Isaiah tells him to set his house in order; the king turns his face to the wall and weeps, pleading his faithfulness. He takes his grief straight to God. Even a settled sentence may be brought before the LORD in prayer.

Before Isaiah leaves the court, God sends him back: I have heard your prayer, seen your tears, and will add fifteen years and defend the city. A poultice of figs heals the boil. God hears the weeping of His servant and graciously reverses what seemed fixed.

Before Isaiah leaves the court, God sends him back: I have heard your prayer, seen your tears, and will add fifteen years and defend the city. A poultice of figs heals the boil. God hears the weeping of His servant and graciously reverses what seemed fixed.

Hezekiah asks a sign, and Isaiah offers the shadow moving forward or back ten degrees; the king chooses the harder, and God turns the shadow backward. The LORD bends even the sun's shadow to confirm His word. No promise of His lacks the power to make itself sure.

Babylon's envoys come with gifts, and Hezekiah proudly shows them all his treasures, holding nothing back. Flattered by attention, he displays his wealth instead of his God. Prosperity and praise can tempt even a godly man to vanity.

Babylon's envoys come with gifts, and Hezekiah proudly shows them all his treasures, holding nothing back. Flattered by attention, he displays his wealth instead of his God. Prosperity and praise can tempt even a godly man to vanity.

Isaiah asks what he showed them; Hezekiah admits, everything. The prophet exposes the folly that pride could not see. We need others to question our choices, for the heart easily hides its own vanity from itself.

Isaiah asks what he showed them; Hezekiah admits, everything. The prophet exposes the folly that pride could not see. We need others to question our choices, for the heart easily hides its own vanity from itself.

Isaiah foretells that all these treasures, and Hezekiah's own sons, will one day be carried to Babylon. The proud display has shown the future plunderer his prize. Sin sown in one generation can ripen into ruin for the next.

Hezekiah answers, 'Good is the word of the LORD,' comforted that peace will last in his own days. His relief is real but a little self-centered. It is a small faith that is content so long as trouble falls only on those who come after.
